item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first two images originate from here, which gives no indication of releasing images under CC.

The last image could not be sourced.

All images have therefore been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt similarly to Hippo. The pacing was good, and while anglerfish analogues are pretty common horror territory, the twist was well-delivered.

I downvoted on aesthetic grounds - I felt that the dialogue, particularly the poetic nature of the debriefing, strained credulity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot to like here; the writing is very solid throughout, and the final twist is clever. This was a solid upvote by the end.

I think, though, that elements of this read too much like a book, and not enough like a report? That's partly an aesthetic choice — and the writing was solid enough for me to be alright with it by the end. But the debriefing never felt like an actual debriefing to me; it felt like the author was trying to tell me a story. The characters in the exploration log also felt a bit too "quirky" for me to buy them as actual people — it felt like a movie script.

Keep in mind, all these criticisms are subjective, and despite my misgivings regarding this reading more like a story than a genuine SCP document, it was very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mixed feelings on this one. Ultimately I think it all came together well enough, but it felt a little on the long and slow side for what it was. The debriefing feels like the heart and soul of the article, but it takes such a long time to get there. I also don't really buy the use of D-Class in the first log, given that it's a routine and reasonably low-risk test where discipline, proper briefing, and familiarity with the abnormal would be pretty major benefits. The specific execution of the twist with the image file didn't do much for me, but I appreciate the idea on a conceptual level.

That said, the debriefing is evocative, dramatic, and tense. The imagery of Hargrove crawling through this ditch for hours, in the rain, with the forest lit up by the orbs and great invisible beasts floating overhead, to conduct the world's most urgent storm repair, is fantastic. Easily enough to earn my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roger wilco that good buddy, 10-4

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's the thing.

I love Kevin Smith-esque dialogue. It's more accessible, more human. Clinical is clinical, but expecting regular subjects to speak and reference things clinically when subjected to the supernatural is unrealistic.

I'm glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it when people make D-bois sound like actual people and not just as clinical as the Foundation members. D-bois will be D-bois.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one who loved the D-boi dialogue? Shit was hilarious.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bingo.

In actuality, the area in real life was underwater long ago. You can still occasionally find the odd shell at random. But yes, this instance is the far, far future in particular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this, my friend, is how you you do a shock image. Well done, with a side of fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like it, or it’s just spatiotemporal, which I’m sure would shock no one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Epic reveal and execution of a real-life thing. Take my up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation is that they're from VERY far in the future, when changing water levels, continental drift, etc. has put the region underwater.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you went one way… and then you took a MASSIVE turn another way. You madman. +1 For the story and creepy factor. Time-based predators? Nice.

But I still don't understand the water aspect. The entire southern US being underwater at the end didn't make any sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really well written SCP. I love the story that goes with this one, at first I thought it would about a few ghost lights, but then I kept reading and reading, going further down the rabbit hole as the whole terrifying truth and implications of the SCP became apparent. Nicely done +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a little concerned when I discovered this was a real-world phenomenon that's just a skip now, but this really ends up being a lot more about the story than the anomaly. Plus, then the anomaly takes a dive at the end.

the instances of 2640 reach

I was confused by this, but otherwise, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like the twilight zone, if the twilight zone had ghost fish and spook tornadoes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, Up until the very end I thought "Yeah, this is a pretty solid, middle-of-the-road skip. Interesting enough."

But that picture is a GOOD damn twist. It's like, we look at angler-fish, and we get how they work and how the little fish they eat get tricked, but there's this underlying assumption of superiority that I've never even thought of before: Obviously, we would never fall for such a trick. The picture totally recontextualizes the whole article by taking our lofty position away. We're the little fish now. Genius. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn your beautiful eye for detail. I ham-handedly fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-1423: Uhhh… looks like… .point-three-two.. H?

Connors: That's 0.32 CentiHumes.

Shouldn't "0.32 H" be either "0.32 Humes" or "32 centiHumes"? Or, alternately, shouldn't the readout say "0.32 cH" instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CRAP. I thought I deleted that. Thank you. It's fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Posting Notes- DrChandra, Monorail crit
Images mine, created using Creative Commons assets
Actually based on a real life unexplained phenomena of the same name- the Gurdon Lights.

For the most part, such information is usually put in the author post in the discussion thread. There is no rule that insists that they cannot be in the main article, but it comes off as jarring when put alongside the in-universe content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't nest a second collapsible, so… it was either that or somehow learn javascript.

Link it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: can't nest collapsibles sadly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that last image: no
just no
why didn't you make it into a collapsible i was starting at that thing for 3 seconds

whatever +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is based on a real-world paranormal phenomenon known as the Gurdon Lights, that I coincidentally live near to. Thanks to DrChandra and Monorail for the crits. Images were created by myself using both self-made and Creative Commons assets.

More info about the real-world lights can be found here-

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gurdon_Light

http://www.encyclopediaofarkansas.net/encyclopedia/entry-detail.aspx?entryID=1198

https://www.arkansas.com/things-to-do/uniquely-arkansas/haunted-arkansas/mysterious-lights/

Enjoy!

EDIT: Changed the non-collapsable image into a collapsible with the help of Phi Fehu, http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/mortos-collapsible and SCP-2999. Thanks to everyone who did stuff to help make my stuff into better stuff.
